section.title: Immanuel, the Sign to Ahaz
section.sectionRationale: This movement opens the great block of Immanuel oracles running through chapters 7-12: Ahaz, terrified by a coalition of Aram and Israel, refuses to ask God for a sign of assurance, so the LORD gives one anyway, a child called Immanuel, God with us, alongside a sober warning that Assyria, the very power Ahaz hopes will save him, will instead overrun the land.
section.thesis: When faithless Ahaz refuses to ask for a sign, the LORD gives one regardless, a virgin-born son called Immanuel, God with us, a near sign of deliverance from two enemy kings that also opens a horizon toward a far greater fulfillment, even as Ahaz's own faithlessness invites the very Assyrian devastation he sought to avoid.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Ahaz's terror before the Aram-Israel coalition; the LORD's offer of a sign and Ahaz's faithless refusal; the sign of Immanuel given anyway; and the coming Assyrian devastation pictured in stripped land and briers.

units.label: Trees shaken by the wind
units.note: Ahaz terrified before Aram and Israel

verses.text: Now in the days that Ahaz son of Jotham, the son of Uzziah, was king of Judah, Rezin king of Aram marched up to wage war against Jerusalem. He was accompanied by Pekah son of Remaliah the king of Israel, but he could not overpower the city.
When it was reported to the house of David that Aram was in league with Ephraim, the hearts of Ahaz and his people trembled like trees in the forest shaken by the wind.
Then the LORD said to Isaiah, “Go out with your son Shear-jashub to meet Ahaz at the end of the aqueduct that feeds the upper pool, on the road to the Launderer's Field,
and say to him: Calm down and be quiet. Do not be afraid or disheartened over these two smoldering stubs of firewood, over the fierce anger of Rezin and Aram and of the son of Remaliah.
For Aram, along with Ephraim and the son of Remaliah, has plotted your ruin, saying:
‘Let us invade Judah, terrorize it, and divide it among ourselves. Then we can install the son of Tabeal over it as king.’

But this is what the Lord GOD says: 
‘It will not arise; it will not happen.
For the head of Aram is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin. Within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered as a people.
The head of Ephraim is Samaria, and the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah. If you do not stand firm in your {{emunah|faith}}, then you will not stand at all.’”

units.label: A sign refused
units.note: I will not test the LORD

verses.text: Again the LORD spoke to Ahaz, saying,
“Ask for a sign from the LORD your God, whether from the depths of Sheol or the heights of heaven.”
But Ahaz replied, “I will not ask; I will not test the LORD.”
Then Isaiah said, “Hear now, O house of David! Is it not enough to try the patience of men? Will you try the patience of my God as well?

units.label: The sign of Immanuel
units.note: the virgin will conceive and bear a son

verses.text: Therefore the Lord Himself will give you a sign: Behold, the {{almah|virgin}} will be with child and will give birth to a son, and will call Him {{immanuel|Immanuel}}.
By the time He knows enough to reject evil and choose good, He will be eating curds and honey.
For before the boy knows enough to reject evil and choose good, the land of the two kings you dread will be laid waste.
The LORD will bring on you and on your people and on the house of your father a time unlike any since the day Ephraim separated from Judah, He will bring the king of Assyria.”

units.label: The coming devastation
units.note: briers, thorns, and a shaved land

verses.text: On that day the LORD will whistle to the flies at the farthest streams of the Nile and to the bees in the land of Assyria.

And they will all come and settle in the steep ravines and clefts of the rocks, 
in all the thornbushes and watering holes.
On that day the Lord will use a razor hired from beyond the Euphrates, the king of Assyria, to shave your head and the hair of your legs, and to remove your beard as well.
On that day a man will raise a young cow and two sheep,
and from the abundance of milk they give, he will eat curds; for all who remain in the land will eat curds and honey.
And on that day, in every place that had a thousand vines worth a thousand shekels of silver, only briers and thorns will be found.
Men will go there with bow and arrow, for the land will be covered with briers and thorns.
For fear of the briers and thorns, you will no longer traverse the hills once tilled by the hoe; they will become places for oxen to graze and sheep to trample.

emunah.gloss: To stand firm, be established, from a root also giving us amen. Verse 9 contains a famous Hebrew wordplay, im lo ta'aminu ki lo te'amenu, if you do not stand firm you will not be established, using the same root twice to make trust itself the very ground of stability.

almah.gloss: A young woman of marriageable age, normally presumed to be unmarried and therefore a virgin in ancient Israelite culture, though the word itself emphasizes youth rather than virginity technically. Matthew 1:23 renders it with the Greek parthenos, virgin, applying the sign fully and finally to Mary's miraculous conception of Jesus.

immanuel.gloss: God with us, a name combining the preposition with us and El, God. The name functions as both a sign of assurance to Ahaz's generation and, in its fullest sense, as the very identity of Christ, who is literally God become flesh and dwelling among His people, Matthew 1:23, John 1:14.

basic.context: King Ahaz is terrified because two neighboring kings have joined forces to attack Jerusalem and replace him with their own puppet ruler. God sends Isaiah to reassure Ahaz that this threat will fail, and even offers Ahaz any sign he wants as proof, but Ahaz refuses under a show of false piety. So God gives a sign anyway: a virgin will bear a son called Immanuel, God with us, and before that child is old enough to know right from wrong, both threatening kings will be gone, though a far more dangerous power, Assyria, whom Ahaz secretly hoped would rescue him, will instead devastate the land.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage operates on two horizons at once, a genuinely historical sign for Ahaz's own crisis around 734 BC, and a further, greater fulfillment that Matthew 1:23 explicitly identifies in the virgin birth of Jesus. The honest question readers must face is how one sign can address both a near, immediate need and a distant, climactic one; the pattern, sometimes called prophetic telescoping, appears elsewhere in Scripture and does not require choosing one fulfillment over the other, since both are affirmed by the text and its New Testament application.
basic.originalLanguages: Verse 9 turns on a famous Hebrew wordplay built on one root, aman, standing firm: if you do not stand firm in faith, you will not be established at all, tying trust itself to stability. The almah, young woman, of verse 14 will bear a son called Immanuel, God with us, a name whose full weight is realized only when Matthew identifies the child as Jesus, literally God dwelling among His people in flesh.
basic.typology: Immanuel, God with us, given first as a sign in Ahaz's crisis, becomes the defining identity of Christ Himself, whose incarnation is the ultimate fulfillment of God's presence among His people. The child eating curds and honey, marking a time of judgment and scarcity, also anticipates a pattern where the Messiah's early life is marked by humble, ordinary circumstances even while carrying the weight of ultimate significance.
basic.christ: Matthew 1:22-23 quotes this prophecy directly, declaring it fulfilled in the virgin conception of Jesus by Mary through the Holy Spirit, and explicitly translating Immanuel as God with us. Where Ahaz refused to trust God even when offered any sign he wanted, Jesus is the sign finally given regardless of human unbelief, God Himself coming to dwell among a faithless people, not because they deserved it but because His mercy would not be stopped by their refusal.
basic.god: God offers grace to Ahaz even in his unbelief, extending an invitation to ask for any sign, and when Ahaz refuses under false humility, God still gives a sign, showing that His purposes of redemption do not depend on human cooperation or worthiness. God is also sovereign over international politics, using and later disciplining the very powers, Assyria, that faithless kings trust in place of Him.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Kings 16:5-9 narrates the historical crisis behind this chapter, including Ahaz's fateful decision to appeal to Assyria for help. Matthew 1:22-23 explicitly cites Isaiah 7:14 as fulfilled in the virgin birth of Jesus. Isaiah 8:8, 10 continues to address the child as Immanuel directly, reinforcing that the name marks God's ongoing presence even amid coming judgment. John 1:14 develops the fullest theological meaning of God dwelling, tabernacling, among His people in the incarnate Christ.
basic.oneStory: The sign of Immanuel given to faithless Ahaz previews the whole gospel pattern: God's presence and rescue given not because people ask rightly or deserve it, but because His mercy breaks through unbelief. That sign finds its full and final meaning in Jesus, God with us in the flesh, come to dwell among a people who, like Ahaz, so often refuse to trust Him.

context.literary: The chapter is a tightly constructed narrative-oracle hybrid: a historical crisis (verses 1-2), a prophetic reassurance and warning (verses 3-9), a refused-yet-given sign (verses 10-17), and an extended judgment oracle pronounced in matching 'on that day' refrains (verses 18-25). The naming of Isaiah's own son, Shear-jashub, a remnant will return, in verse 3 quietly reinforces the chapter's theological point before Ahaz even speaks.
context.historical: This is the Syro-Ephraimite crisis of around 734 BC, when Rezin of Aram (Damascus) and Pekah of Israel (Ephraim) allied against Judah, seeking to force Ahaz into a coalition against Assyria's expansion. Rather than trusting God's assurance, Ahaz appealed directly to Tiglath-Pileser III of Assyria for help, 2 Kings 16:7-9, a decision that brought short-term relief but long-term vassalage and the devastation this very chapter warns about.
context.canonical: Isaiah 7:14 is among the most significant messianic texts in the Old Testament, cited directly in Matthew 1:23 as fulfilled in Christ's virgin birth, and it opens the larger block of Immanuel material running through chapters 7-12 that develops the child's identity further in the Wonderful Counselor of 9:6 and the shoot from Jesse in 11:1.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reassure Ahaz and the house of David that the immediate coalition threat would fail, to expose Ahaz's unbelief in refusing a sign, and to give a sign that would address both the immediate crisis and, in its fullest sense, the ultimate hope of God's presence with His people in the Messiah.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage narrates a specific historical episode and a specific prophetic sign; it does not prescribe that believers should generally refuse or demand signs from God. The enduring principle is that unbelief dressed as piety, I will not test the Lord, still amounts to refusing to trust God, and that God's redemptive purposes advance regardless of human faithlessness.

debates.question: Does Isaiah 7:14 refer only to a child born in Ahaz's own day, only to Christ's virgin birth centuries later, or to both?

views.label: A near, historical fulfillment in Isaiah's day
views.case: Isaiah 8:3-4 describes a child born to Isaiah's own wife in a similar timeframe, and almah, young woman, need not mean a miraculous virgin conception; the sign's timing, before the boy knows right from wrong, fits an imminent, near-term child whose growth marks the collapse of the two threatening kings.

views.label: Fulfilled ultimately and fully in Christ's virgin birth
views.case: Matthew 1:22-23 explicitly and directly applies this prophecy to Jesus's virgin conception, using the Greek parthenos, virgin, and only a miraculous virgin birth could function as the extraordinary 'sign' language in verse 14 implies, since an ordinary young woman's ordinary pregnancy would hardly qualify as a sign to a skeptical king.

views.label: Both, in a pattern of near and far fulfillment
views.case: Many interpreters hold both together: a child born in Isaiah's own generation served as the immediate sign to Ahaz, while the ultimate, climactic fulfillment awaited Christ, the pattern of prophetic telescoping seen elsewhere where near events preview greater ones.

debates.bearing: Whichever combination one holds, the text's own trajectory and Matthew's inspired application affirm that this prophecy's deepest and truest meaning is realized in Jesus, God with us in the flesh, without needing to deny a possible, more immediate first-level fulfillment for Ahaz's own generation.

study.originalLanguages: Verse 9 hinges on a wordplay from the single root aman: im lo ta'aminu ki lo te'amenu, if you do not stand firm you will not be established, so that trust and stability share the very same Hebrew word. The almah, young woman, of verse 14 conceives and names her son Immanuel, immanu El, God with us, a name whose weight Matthew's Gospel unpacks fully by translating it directly and applying it to Jesus, Emmanuel, God with us in the flesh.

typology.type: Immanuel, God with us, given as a sign to Ahaz, Isaiah 7:14
typology.fulfillment: Jesus Christ, God incarnate, dwelling among His people, Matthew 1:23, John 1:14.

typology.type: The child eating curds and honey amid judgment, Isaiah 7:15
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of the Messiah entering humble, ordinary human circumstances even while carrying ultimate redemptive significance, Philippians 2:6-8.

study.christ: Matthew 1:22-23 quotes Isaiah 7:14 directly as fulfilled in Jesus, born of the virgin Mary through the Holy Spirit, and translates Immanuel plainly as God with us, identifying Jesus as the definitive, final answer to this sign. Where faithless Ahaz refused even to ask for assurance, God gave the sign anyway; in the same way, God gave His Son, the ultimate Immanuel, not because humanity asked rightly or deserved it, but because His redemptive purpose would not be thwarted by unbelief.
study.god: God graciously offers Ahaz any sign he could imagine, showing His willingness to meet human doubt with evidence, yet when Ahaz refuses under a cloak of false piety, God still keeps His word and gives a sign regardless, proving His purposes never hinge on human cooperation. He is equally sovereign over the great powers of the ancient world, using Assyria both as an instrument of discipline against faithless Judah and, later in the book, as an empire He Himself will judge in turn.

crossReferences.note: Narrates the historical Syro-Ephraimite crisis and Ahaz's fateful appeal to Assyria instead of trusting the LORD.
Continues to address the coming judgment directly to 'Immanuel,' reinforcing God's presence even amid devastation.
Explicitly cites Isaiah 7:14 as fulfilled in the virgin conception of Jesus.
Develops the fullest theological meaning of God dwelling, tabernacling, among His people in the incarnate Christ.

study.oneStory: The sign of Immanuel given to faithless Ahaz previews the whole gospel pattern: God's presence and rescue offered not because people ask rightly or deserve it, but because His mercy breaks through unbelief to accomplish His purposes anyway. That sign reaches its full and final meaning in Jesus, God with us in the flesh, come to dwell among a people who, like Ahaz, so often refuse to trust Him.

quiz.q: Why were Ahaz and his people so terrified according to verses 1-2?

options.text: Aram and Israel had allied together to attack Jerusalem and replace Ahaz with a puppet king.
options.note: This is the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, a real, historical political and military threat.

options.text: A plague had broken out in Jerusalem.
options.note: The threat described is a military coalition, not disease.

options.text: Assyria had already conquered Judah.
options.note: Assyria's devastation is warned about later in the chapter as a future consequence, not yet an accomplished conquest.

options.text: Uzziah had just died, leaving no heir.
options.note: Uzziah's death is the setting of chapter 6; here Ahaz, his grandson, is already reigning and threatened by the Aram-Israel coalition.

quiz.q: How does Ahaz respond when invited to ask God for any sign he wants?

options.text: He refuses, dressing his unbelief up as pious reluctance to test the LORD.
options.note: Isaiah exposes this as trying the patience of God, not genuine humility.

options.text: He eagerly asks for a sign from the depths of Sheol.
options.note: Ahaz explicitly refuses to ask for any sign at all.

options.text: He asks Isaiah to consult other prophets instead.
options.note: The text records a direct refusal, not a request for alternate counsel.

options.text: He accepts but demands a delay of several years.
options.note: Ahaz's response is an outright refusal, not a request for delay.

quiz.q: What does the name Immanuel mean, and why is it significant?

options.text: God with us, a name that ultimately identifies Jesus Christ Himself as God dwelling among His people.
options.note: Matthew 1:23 explicitly translates and applies this name directly to Christ.

options.text: It means mighty warrior and refers only to a future military leader.
options.note: The name's actual meaning is God with us, not a military title.

options.text: It is simply a common name with no theological significance.
options.note: The name is theologically loaded throughout Isaiah 7-8 and climactically in Matthew's Gospel.

options.text: It refers exclusively to the return from Babylonian exile.
options.note: While some see a near fulfillment in Isaiah's own day, the name's fullest, New Testament-confirmed meaning centers on Christ's incarnation.

quiz.q: What irony does the chapter's ending reveal about Ahaz's unbelief?

options.text: The very Assyrian power Ahaz would later turn to for help would instead devastate his own land.
options.note: 2 Kings 16:7-9 records Ahaz's actual appeal to Assyria, which brought long-term subjugation rather than lasting rescue.

options.text: Aram and Israel eventually became Judah's permanent allies.
options.note: The text says these two kings and their threat would come to nothing, not that they became allies.

options.text: Judah's population grew dramatically as a result of Ahaz's decisions.
options.note: The described outcome is devastation, briers, and thorns, not population growth.

options.text: Ahaz eventually repented and trusted the LORD fully.
options.note: The historical record and this chapter's warnings suggest ongoing faithlessness rather than full repentance.

quiz.q: What does verse 9's wordplay, if you do not stand firm, you will not be established, emphasize?

options.text: That trust in God is itself the very foundation of genuine stability and security.
options.note: The shared Hebrew root ties belief and stability together linguistically and theologically.

options.text: That military strength alone determines a nation's survival.
options.note: The verse ties survival to faith, not to military might.

options.text: That Ahaz's political alliances would guarantee Judah's safety.
options.note: The verse challenges reliance on political maneuvering, calling instead for trust in God.

options.text: That prophecy always requires a visible sign to be believed.
options.note: The verse is a call to trust apart from demanding proof, the opposite of requiring visible confirmation first.

questions: Ahaz refused to ask God for a sign, dressing unbelief up as piety. Where might you be avoiding trust in God under the guise of not wanting to 'test' Him?
God gave the sign of Immanuel even though Ahaz refused to ask for it. How does it change you to know that God's promises do not depend on your asking rightly or deserving them?

Ahaz eventually turned to Assyria instead of trusting the LORD, and that choice brought long-term trouble he was trying to avoid. What 'Assyria' are you tempted to lean on instead of trusting God right now?
The name Immanuel, God with us, was first a sign of comfort in crisis. Where do you most need to remember that God is genuinely with you in your present fear or uncertainty?
Verse 9 ties standing firm to believing firmly. What would it look like this week to let your trust in God, rather than your circumstances, be the ground of your stability?

translationNotes.note: The wordplay ta'aminu, you believe or stand firm, and te'amenu, you will be established, both from the same root aman, is largely untranslatable into English, though several versions attempt it with phrases like 'stand firm... not stand at all.'

Almah denotes a young woman of marriageable age, generally presumed unmarried and thus a virgin in the cultural context, though it is not the more technical Hebrew term betulah; the Septuagint's Greek translators nonetheless rendered it parthenos, virgin, centuries before Christ, and Matthew 1:23 follows that translation in applying it to Mary.

The imagery of a hired razor shaving head, legs, and beard pictures total, humiliating devastation, since shaving in this culture, apart from specific rituals, was associated with shame and mourning, and here it symbolizes Assyria stripping Judah bare.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from the Makor scripture toolkit for Isaiah 7:1-25. Where BSB printed em dashes they have been rendered as commas per house style.
